hero_headline: A New Year Reality Check: Where AI Helps, Where It Hurts, and How to Tell the Difference
intro: Welcome to the February issue. We are a month into 2026, and the headlines have swung from hype to a healthier dose of caution. This month leans into that balance. You will find the trends worth watching this year, the tools actually saving teachers time, and a few honest warnings about where AI in schools can go sideways. Read the wins, but read the watch-outs too.

items.title: AI in K-12: A New Year Reality Check for School Leaders
items.takeaway: A grounded look at what AI can and cannot do for districts in 2026, written for leaders who have to make real decisions rather than chase the hype.

items.title: 6 Trends to Watch for K-12 in 2026
items.takeaway: A quick map of where K-12 is heading this year so you can plan ahead instead of reacting later.

items.title: Heightened AI Use in Special Education Brings Elevated Risks
items.takeaway: AI can help with IEPs and accommodations, but the privacy and accuracy stakes are higher in special education, and this lays out what to watch under IDEA and Section 504.

items.title: 10 Useful Tech Tools for Educators in 2026: A Practical Guide
items.takeaway: A practical, no-fluff list of ten tools teachers can actually use this year, with the why for each one.

items.title: AI Tools for Teachers That Drive Efficiencies
items.takeaway: Where AI is genuinely saving teachers time, so you can point staff toward the tools that lighten the load instead of adding to it.

items.title: Microsoft Innovations to Support AI-Powered Teaching and Learning
items.takeaway: New Microsoft features for teaching and learning, worth a look since this is our Copilot environment and these land in tools we already use.

sections.name: Proceed With Caution
sections.note: A standing spot for the honest watch-outs. AI is a tool, not a shortcut around judgment.

items.title: Report: The Risks of AI in Schools Outweigh the Benefits
items.takeaway: A report arguing the current risks of AI in schools outweigh the payoff, and a useful gut check before we lean in too hard.

items.title: Denver Schools Blocking ChatGPT for Students
items.takeaway: Denver blocked ChatGPT for students over new features like group chat and planned adult content, a real-world example of why student-facing access needs guardrails.

editor_pick.title: AI in K-12: A New Year Reality Check for School Leaders
editor_pick.blurb: If you read one thing this month, make it this. It cuts through the noise and frames AI the way leaders actually need it framed: useful in places, risky in others, and never a substitute for judgment.
